chunks.text: So let's crank this up a notch and start looking at

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: For those that are not familiar with what a median is,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: if you were to have a list of numbers and then you are to sort them in order,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: the median is the middle number.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: So in this case, when we have these seven numbers right here,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: six is the middle number.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: Whereas when we have eight numbers,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: we take the average between the fourth and fifth number,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: which can be like four and five,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: and that becomes 4.5.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: Now median is extremely important, especially when you're working with data.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: In this case, we're looking at salary distribution.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: This is from my Python course.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: And we're looking at salary distributions.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: And you can see that we have the salary go up and then go down.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: But then it goes out and we have like high out values,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: way out past three hundred and fifty thousand.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: If we just use the average,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: this average is going to be pushed towards a higher number

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: and is not going to be representistic of the actual data.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: So median helps fix this issue by basically sorting all those numbers,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: taking that middle number and getting a more

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: representistic number of what you would expect to see.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: In this case, salary, what would you expect to see?

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: A one, one expect to see.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: A higher salary when I know I'm going to get a lower.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: So you may be like, this is pretty easy.

chunks.text: All I got to do is change all these average values to median

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: and then run this.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: Unfortunately, there's no such function as median.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate

chunks.text: whether you're using Postgres SQL Server or MySQL,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: we're going to use this percentile cont or continuous.

chunks.text: And that continuous portion is a key part

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: because now this function that we're using percentile cont

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: is not only an aggregate function,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: but it's an ordered set aggregate function.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: So what does this all mean?

chunks.text: Not only do we have to use percentile cont

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: and what fraction we want to use for this,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: in this case, the median or half of it is 0.5,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: but we also have to use this other syntax here

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: of within group and then order by.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: So let's actually break this syntax down.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: Using this percentile continuous function,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: we need to provide it basically a list of ordered values.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: It's not going to sort it itself,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: and build a pick it out like other aggregate functions.

chunks.text: Because of this, we first, in parentheses,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: have this order by column, this specifies

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: how we want to order the values

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: that we're going to be picking out the median value from.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: But this only sorts the values.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: That's why we have this within group portion right here

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: to bind that ordered set, if you will, to that function.

chunks.text: We're going to be segmenting based on where an order falls within its percentile.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: Well, it's actually pretty common in statistics to use these values

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: in order to bucket things into their different quartiles.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: Technically, statisticians like to call that range between 25 and 75 the entire quartile range,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: we're just going to call it medium.

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: And then everything less than this, low and everything higher than this, high.

chunks.text: So that's the basis on where we're getting these numbers for.

chunks.text: So let's get into breaking this down. We're going to be using these keywords of explain

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: and explain analyze for each of these. They just go at the beginning of a SQL command,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: whether what you're using, but what's the difference between these two? Well, explain

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: demonstrates the execution plan without actually executing it. Whereas explain analyze, basically,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: means like it's going to analyze it and it actually does execute it. So we understand what the

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: execution times are. So say we have this simple query of select star from sales. I could use explain

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: at the beginning of this running command enter. And it's going to tell me the query plan. I'll

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: break this down in a second. Now, we also could use something like explain analyze, right? And

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: remember, this one is one row when I run explain analyze. We have two more rows. Mainly, this one has

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: the execution time. It has well, none of the planning time, but also execution time. So you may be

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: like, look, when the heck would I use explain and when would I use explain analyze? Like, why would I

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: want this if this doesn't even tell me the execution time? Well, let's say you're working with an

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: extremely large database like millions or even billions of rows. It could be extremely cumbersome

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: to run this query and cost a lot of money, not only time, but also money. So there may be cases

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: that we would only want to use explain. But since this database we're working with is so small,

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: we're going to always be running explain analyze with all the queries we do. That way we can also

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: see the execution time. So let's break this down on what this is actually providing. So this first

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: row here says that it does a sec scan, which means it does a sequential scan basically going row by row

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: by row. And it specifies that it's doing this on the sales. Then from there, we have three variables

chunks.title: SQL for Data Analytics – Intermediate
chunks.text: inside of parentheses here. Cost is just an arbitrary unit. And that's just assigned by

chunks.text: hostgres. You want to be real about it. It just made it up. But quantity wise, it remains consistent.
